Standard Name/Number: Governance for Augmented Intelligence Toolkit
Summary: Step‑by‑step organizational AI governance guidance: form a cross‑functional working group, assess current policies, define project intake and vendor evaluation, incorporate oversight/monitoring, and embed change management.
Healthcare Implications: Designed for health systems and medical groups to operationalize AI governance in practice. Provides templates/processes to evaluate GenAI and predictive tools for safety, equity, privacy, and workflow fit.

Standard Name/Number: RSNA Statement on the Application of AI in Radiology
Summary: Radiological Society of North America articulates principles for responsible AI in radiology, including safety and effectiveness, bias mitigation, transparency, and education. Highlights RSNA’s role in certification and training resources and encourages rigorous evaluation and ongoing monitoring.
Healthcare Implications: Radiology groups can map local AI governance (selection, deployment, monitoring) to the statement’s principles. Useful for credentialing, privileging, and quality committees overseeing imaging AI.

Standard Name/Number: AI Code of Conduct for Health, Health Care, and Biomedical Science
Summary: National Academy of Medicine's multi‑stakeholder initiative to articulate a health‑sector AI Code of Conduct spanning accuracy, safety, reliability, equity, and ethics. Includes publications and ongoing work toward implementable guardrails for AI in health and biomedical science.
Healthcare Implications: Offers a sector‑specific reference for leadership teams aligning AI programs to shared expectations. Complements National Institute of Standards and Technology AI Risk Management Framework by focusing on clinical quality, patient safety, and equity commitments.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Standard Name/Number: Good Machine Learning Practice for Medical Device Development: Guiding Principles
Summary: AHA’s first scientific statement on AI in cardiovascular care outlines priorities for data quality (representativeness, provenance), transparency and validation, clinical trial design and reporting for AI tools, workflow integration, and post‑deployment monitoring. It calls for multidisciplinary governance, rigorous external validation, and bias assessment across the Computer Vision care continuum (imaging, risk prediction, triage, and therapeutics).
Healthcare Implications: Cardiology programs using AI (e.g., Computer Vision imaging triage, risk models) can map local governance and study designs to the statement: require dataset documentation, bias audits, prospective evaluation before scale‑up, and ongoing performance surveillance with human oversight and clear accountability.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government; Patients & Public

Standard Name/Number: Characterization Considerations for Medical Device Software and Software-Specific Risk
Summary: ANA’s position statement frames the ethical use of AI through core nursing values and the Code of Ethics, emphasizing patient dignity, privacy, informed consent, accountability, and the nurse’s duty to advocate. It cautions against AI uses that erode the therapeutic relationship and urges education, competency, and organizational policies for safe AI use.
Healthcare Implications: Nursing leadership can translate the statement into practice by defining nurse responsibilities when using AI tools (documentation aids, clinical decision support), ensuring transparency to patients, establishing escalation pathways for algorithmic errors, and embedding AI ethics into competency and training programs.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems

Standard Name/Number: The Playbook: Implementing AI in Healthcare
Summary: Consensus resources to prepare health systems for responsible, scalable AI implementation in clinical care. Includes readiness assessment, governance models, and return on investments considerations for sustainable adoption.
Healthcare Implications: Provides stepwise templates for clinical, technical, and operational readiness; helps frame business cases and success metrics for AI in care delivery.

Issuing Body: Healthcare Information and Management Systems Society
Standard Name/Number: AI Policy Principles for Health AI
Summary: Principles to promote responsible governance and deployment of Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning in healthcare, emphasizing safety, accountability, transparency, privacy, interoperability, innovation, and workforce development.
Healthcare Implications: Policymakers and provider executives can align internal policies and advocacy efforts with these principles. Supports program‑level key performance indicators for ongoing model monitoring and re‑validation.

Standard Name/Number: HITRUST AI Risk Management Insights Report
Summary: Standardized reporting artifact generated from the AI RM Assessment summarizing control posture and mappings (e.g., to National Institute of Standards and TechnologyAI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF), International Organization for Standardization/International Electrotechnical Commission 23894). Intended for stakeholders (boards, customers, partners) to review AI risk management activities and planned mitigations.
Healthcare Implications: Useful for sharing AI assurance evidence with provider organizations, group purchasing organizations, and payers. Helps clinical stakeholders interpret an external vendor’s AI governance maturity.

Standard Name/Number: Artificial Intelligence in the Provision of Health Care
Summary: ACP recommends that AI augment – not replace – physician decision‑making, and outlines expectations for evidence, transparency, privacy, liability, and equity. Urges rigorous evaluation and ongoing monitoring before widespread clinical use.
Healthcare Implications: Internal medicine practices can adopt the recommendations as guardrails for selecting and governing AI in ambulatory and inpatient settings. Useful for medical staff policy updates and privileging criteria.

Issuing Body: College of Healthcare Information Management Executives
Standard Name/Number: CHIME 10 AI Principles for Healthcare
Summary: Ten principles for safe, equitable, and effective health‑system AI covering governance, patient safety, privacy/security, transparency, interoperability, and workforce. Oriented to executive‑level decision‑making.
Healthcare Implications: Useful for CIO/CMIO‑led AI governance councils to align strategy, set evaluation criteria for enterprise AI, and inform policy/education efforts.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias

Issuing Body: National Institute of Standards and Technology
Standard Name/Number: NIST AI 600-1 – NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile
Summary: A cross‑sector profile of the National Institute of Standards and Technology AI Risk Management Framework tailored to Generative AI. Maps GenAI‑specific risks (e.g., hallucinations, prompt injection, privacy leakage) to concrete actions across RMF functions, with implementation examples and references.
Healthcare Implications: Useful for governing GenAI uses like patient‑facing chat, ambient documentation, summarization, and knowledge assistants. Supports controls for safety, privacy, and content provenance in clinical workflows and staff‑facing tools.

Standard Name/Number: HITRUST AI Assurance Program & AI Risk Management Assessment
Summary: Assessment aligned to National Institute of Standards and Technology AI Risk Management Framework and International Organization for Standardization/International Electrotechnical Commission 23894 that evaluates AI-specific risks across the lifecycle and produces an Insights Report. Organizations can self-assess or work with a HITRUST assessor through MyCSF to document controls and remediation plans. Intended to evidence AI risk governance to boards, partners, and regulators.
Healthcare Implications: Health systems and vendors can use the assessment to baseline governance of clinical and operational AI (e.g., documentation, monitoring, third‑party risk). Outputs support procurement diligence and payer/provider assurance; complements internal model inventories and risk registers.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Standard Name/Number: Use of Artificial Intelligence in Improving Outcomes in Heart Disease
Summary: Evidence-based review of AI applications across cardiovascular care (diagnostics, imaging, risk prediction, decision support), with recommendations for validation, equity, and implementation. Emphasizes outcome monitoring and clinician oversight.
Healthcare Implications: Cardiology services can use the statement to guide evaluation and deployment of AI tools (e.g., ECG algorithms, imaging analysis). Highlights transparency, bias assessment, and clinical impact evaluation as prerequisites for adoption.

Standard Name/Number: Joint Multi-Society Statement on AI in Radiology
Summary: Practical considerations for developing, purchasing, implementing, and monitoring AI tools in radiology, covering governance, validation, workflow integration, and post‑market performance tracking. Provides checklists and operational recommendations.
Healthcare Implications: Actionable reference for imaging departments to structure AI procurement and QA programs; aligns with American College of Radiology ARCH‑AI concepts and Radiological Society of North America guidance.

Standard Name/Number: Regulatory Considerations on Artificial Intelligence for Health
Summary: American College of Radiology’s ARCH‑AI is a voluntary quality‑assurance recognition program for radiology sites using AI. It operationalizes best practices across governance, data management, validation, workflow integration, monitoring, and safety reporting to attest that imaging AI is implemented responsibly in clinical practice.
Healthcare Implications: Radiology departments can pursue ARCH‑AI recognition to structure AI governance and monitoring, standardize QA across AI tools, strengthen vendor requirements, and support payer/credentialing discussions about safe, effective use of imaging AI.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Safety & Risk; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Patients & Public

Standard Name/Number: ARCH-AI – ACR Recognized Center for Healthcare AI
Summary: American College of Radiology’s ARCH‑AI program recognizes radiology facilities that implement best practices for AI governance, infrastructure, and monitoring in imaging interpretation. Outlines consensus‑based building blocks and provides assessment for recognition.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a pathway for radiology departments to systematize AI QA, improve safety/effectiveness tracking, and communicate readiness to patients and partners.

Standard Name/Number: Validation Framework for the Use of Artificial Intelligence Within Healthcare – Specification
Summary: ACP’s policy paper sets physician‑centric principles for AI adoption: AI should augment – not replace – clinical judgment; maintain patient autonomy and privacy; be transparent about data sources and limitations; and be evaluated for safety, efficacy, and equity before and after deployment. It recommends research standards, liability clarity, and access equity.
Healthcare Implications: Health systems can adopt ACP’s principles as guardrails for privileging/credentialing of AI‑assisted care, procurement criteria (evidence, explainability, bias mitigation), and documentation of physician oversight and accountability in AI‑supported decisions.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Standard Name/Number: Health AI Partnership Guides & Health AI Hub
Summary: Practical, evergreen guides for healthcare delivery organizations on safe, effective, and equitable AI adoption. Curates case studies and how‑tos across procurement, validation, implementation, and monitoring.
Healthcare Implications: Focuses on provider operational realities – clinical workflow integration, change management, and evaluation. Helps teams translate high‑level principles into day‑to‑day implementation practices.

Standard Name/Number: Blueprint for Trustworthy AI Implementation Guidance for Healthcare
Summary: Consensus recommendations from health systems, academia, industry, and government for trustworthy AI in healthcare. Covers assurance, transparency, measurement and monitoring, and lifecycle governance; includes practical artifacts and checklists.
Healthcare Implications: Useful for hospital AI councils and product teams to define assurance evidence (pre‑deployment through post‑market monitoring). Can be mapped to regulatory expectations (e.g., U.S. Food and Drug Administration Predetermined Change Control Plan concepts) and enterprise risk controls.

Issuing Body: Association for the Advancement of Medical Instrumentation
Standard Name/Number: AAMI TIR34971 – 2023 Application of ISO 14971 to ML in AI
Summary: Companion to International Organization for Standardization 14971:2019 focused on risk management for machine learning/AI medical devices, including hazard identification, risk controls, verification/validation, and documentation practices tailored to ML characteristics (data drift, model updates).
Healthcare Implications: Widely adopted by device developers and hospital innovators working under quality systems; supports Predetermined Change Control Plan‑style change planning and Good Machine Learning Practice alignment.

Issuing Body: National Institute of Standards and Technology
Standard Name/Number: AI RMF 1.0 – NIST AI Risk Management Framework
Summary: Voluntary, sector-agnostic framework (Identify, Measure, Manage, Govern) to help organizations manage AI risks across the lifecycle. Includes core functions, categories, and informative references; supported by an online Playbook, Profiles, and crosswalks to other standards.
Healthcare Implications: Hospitals, payers, and vendors can use the RMF to structure governance for clinical and operational AI – e.g., risk registers, impact assessments, model cards, and continuous monitoring. Aligns well with health‑specific guidance (e.g., HTI‑1 DSI transparency) and can be profiled for care settings.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Standard Name/Number: Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence in Nursing Practice
Summary: Sets ethical expectations for AI in nursing: preserve human relationships, ensure safety/competence, maintain privacy, and address bias. Calls for nursing involvement in design, deployment, and evaluation of AI systems impacting practice.
Healthcare Implications: Guides hospital nursing leadership on acceptable AI uses (e.g., ambient documentation, staffing tools), emphasizing training, transparency, and reporting mechanisms for harms or malfunctions.

Standard Name/Number: IEEE 7003-2022 – Algorithmic Bias Considerations
Summary: Provides processes and methodologies to address bias in the creation and deployment of algorithmic systems, including dataset selection, validation criteria, documentation, and communication of application boundaries.
Healthcare Implications: Directly applicable to clinical algorithm development and deployment (subgroup analysis, drift monitoring, performance reporting). Supports governance requirements in health‑equity and civil‑rights policies (e.g., Office for Civil Rights §1557 clinical algorithms).

Standard Name/Number: IEEE 7002-2022 – Data Privacy Process
Summary: Specifies a systems/software engineering process for privacy-by-design, including requirements elicitation, data minimization, consent, and lifecycle controls. Applies to products and services processing personal data, including AI systems.
Healthcare Implications: Useful for digital health apps, remote monitoring platforms, and clinical AI tools processing PHI/PII. Supports privacy impact assessments and engineering controls aligned to healthcare compliance expectations.

Issuing Body: National Institute of Standards and Technology
Standard Name/Number: NIST SP 1270 – Identifying and Managing Bias in AI
Summary: Provides a structured approach to identifying and managing bias in AI systems, spanning data, human, and systemic sources. Addresses terminology, measurement, and evaluation practices to mitigate harmful outcomes.
Healthcare Implications: Directly applicable to clinical algorithm development and validation (e.g., subgroup performance, fairness metrics). Helps health systems design bias controls and documentation for oversight bodies and quality governance.

Standard Name/Number: IEEE 7001-2021 – Transparency of Autonomous Systems
Summary: Defines requirements for achieving and documenting transparency in autonomous/intelligent systems, including traceability, explainability, and communication to stakeholders. Supports accountability and human oversight by clarifying what information should be available and when.
Healthcare Implications: Applicable to AI-enabled clinical tools (e.g., imaging triage, decision support) to structure explainability artifacts and user communications. Helps providers evaluate vendor claims and set expectations for documentation and post‑market updates.

Issuing Body: U.S. FDA; Health Canada; UK MHRA
Standard Name/Number: Good Machine Learning Practice for Medical Device Development
Summary: Tri‑agency principles for machine learning‑enabled medical devices covering good practices across the total product lifecycle (data, training, testing, deployment, and ongoing learning) to promote safety, effectiveness, and quality.
Healthcare Implications: Device developers and clinical collaborators can use the principles as scaffolding for validation, change management, and transparency – especially for continuously learning models.

Issuing Body: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services
Standard Name/Number: Trustworthy AI Playbook
Summary: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Office of the Chief AI Officer playbook outlining trustworthy AI concepts, building blocks, and project lifecycle considerations to support ethical, effective, and secure AI solutions across the Department.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a reference for U.S. Department of Health and Human Services‑funded entities and partners; concepts map to governance artifacts (e.g., risk assessments, monitoring plans) used by health systems and vendors working with federal programs.

Standard Name/Number: Ethics and Governance of Artificial Intelligence for Health
Summary: CHIME’s AI Principles articulate CIO‑led expectations for responsible AI in health systems – covering governance, accountability, transparency, privacy/security, workforce readiness, vendor management, and equity – aimed at safe, value‑driven deployment of AI.
Healthcare Implications: Useful to shape enterprise AI governance charters and procurement checklists: require model documentation from vendors, privacy and security controls, bias/impact assessments, change‑control for model updates, and workforce training plans.
Keyword Tags: Equity & Bias; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors
